subject: Physics
q: A square loop PQRS having 10 turns, area $3.6 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}^2$ and resistance $100 \Omega$ is slowly and uniformly being pulled out of a uniform magnetic field of magnitude $\mathrm{B}=0.5 \mathrm{~T}$ as shown. Work done in pulling the loop out of the field in $1.0 \mathrm{~s}$ is ______ $\times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~J}$.
question: A square loop PQRS having 10 turns, area $3.6 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}^2$ and resistance $100 \Omega$ is slowly and uniformly being pulled out of a uniform magnetic field of magnitude $\mathrm{B}=0.5 \mathrm{~T}$ as shown. Work done in pulling the loop out of the field in $1.0 \mathrm{~s}$ is ______ $\times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~J}$.
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \epsilon=N B \ell v \\ & i=\frac{\epsilon}{R}=\frac{N B \ell v}{R} \\ & F=N(i \ell B)=\frac{N^2 B^2 \ell^2 v}{R}\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{W}=\mathrm{F} \times \ell=\frac{\mathrm{N}^2 \mathrm{~B}^2 \ell^3}{\mathrm{R}}\left(\frac{\ell}{\mathrm{t}}\right) \\ & \mathrm{A}=\ell^2 \\ & \mathrm{~W}=\frac{(10 \times 10)(0.5)^2 \times\left(3.6 \times 10^{-3}\right)^2}{100 \times 1} \\ & \mathrm{~W}=3.24 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~J}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q:
The source of time varying magnetic field may be

(A) a permanent magnet

(B) an electric field changing linearly with time

(C) direct current

(D) a decelerating charge particle

(E) an antenna fed with a digital signal

Choose the correct answer from the options given below.
question:
The source of time varying magnetic field may be

(A) a permanent magnet

(B) an electric field changing linearly with time

(C) direct current

(D) a decelerating charge particle

(E) an antenna fed with a digital signal

Choose the correct answer from the options given below.
solution:
An electric field varying linearly with time will not produce variable magnetic field as current will be constant.

A decelerating charged particle can result in a time varying magnetic field by emitting radiation.

Similarly a permanent magnet has a constant magnetic field and a direct current does not change its direction and cannot produce a magnetic field.

An antenna can modulate the digital signals .

So, the right option is the statement D.

subject: Physics
q: A thin strip 10 cm long is on a U shaped wire of negligible resistance and it is connected to a spring of spring constant 0.5 N m-1 (see figure). The assembly is kept in a uniform magnetic field of 0.1 T. If the strip is pulled from its equilibrium position and released, the number of oscillations it performs before its amplitude decreases by a factor of e is N . If the mass of the strip is 50 grams, its resistance 10Ω and air drag negligible, N will be close to:
question: A thin strip 10 cm long is on a U shaped wire of negligible resistance and it is connected to a spring of spring constant 0.5 N m-1 (see figure). The assembly is kept in a uniform magnetic field of 0.1 T. If the strip is pulled from its equilibrium position and released, the number of oscillations it performs before its amplitude decreases by a factor of e is N . If the mass of the strip is 50 grams, its resistance 10Ω and air drag negligible, N will be close to:
solution:
Equation of motion for slider:
-md2xdt2=kx+ilB
md2xdt2+kx+BlvRlB=0 ⇒md2xdt2+B2l2R.dxdt+kx=0
Comparing with md2xdt2+b.dxdt+kx=0
m=m ;b=B2l2R;k=k
We know: A=A0e-b2m×t=A0e-B2l22mR×t
For amplitude to be A0/e,
A0e-B2l22mR×t=Ae⇒B2l22mR×t=1
t=2mRB2l2=10,000 sec
Time period = 2πmk=2 s
Number of oscillations = 10,0002=5,000

subject: Physics
q: An insulated copper wire of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn></math> turns is wrapped around a wooden cylindrical core of the cross-sectional area <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>24</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi>cm</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math>. The two ends of the wire are connected to a resistor. The total resistance in the circuit is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>12</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math>. If an externally applied uniform magnetic field in the core along its axis changes from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math> in one direction to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math> in the opposite direction, the charge flowing through a point in the circuit during the change of magnetic field will be _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>mC</mi></math>.
question: An insulated copper wire of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn></math> turns is wrapped around a wooden cylindrical core of the cross-sectional area <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>24</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi>cm</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math>. The two ends of the wire are connected to a resistor. The total resistance in the circuit is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>12</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math>. If an externally applied uniform magnetic field in the core along its axis changes from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math> in one direction to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math> in the opposite direction, the charge flowing through a point in the circuit during the change of magnetic field will be _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>mC</mi></math>.
solution:
The induced emf ε in the turns of the coil can be written as

ε=-dφdt=iR   ...1

Simplify equation (1) to obtain the required charge through a point in the circuit.

-dφdt=iR⇒-1R∫φ1φ2dφ=∫idt⇒q=φ1-φ2R   ...2

Where, φ1, φ2 are the magnetic flux associated with the coil from two ends.

As, φ=NBA, from equation (2), it can be written that

q=NARB1-B2   ...3

where, N, A are the number of turns and the cross-sectional area of the core respectively.

Substitute the values of the known parameters into equation (3) to calculate the required charge.

q=100×24×10-4 m2×1.5 T--1.5 T12 Ω=0.06 C×1000 mC1 C= 60 mC

subject: Physics
q: As per the given figure, if dIdt=-1 A s-1, then the value of VAB at this instant will be ______ V.
question: As per the given figure, if dIdt=-1 A s-1, then the value of VAB at this instant will be ______ V.
solution:
Given here, dIdt=-1 A s-1

Applying Kirchhoff's voltage law to the circuit,

VA-IR-LdIdt-12=VB

⇒VA-2×12-6(-1)-12=VB

⇒VAB=VA-VB=36-6=30 V

q: A square loop of side <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> and resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>7</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> is placed vertically in the east-west plane. A uniform magnetic field of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math> is set up across the plane in the north-east direction. The magnetic field is decreased to zero in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> at a steady rate. Then, the magnitude of induced emf is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msqrt><mi>x</mi></msqrt><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is _______.
question: A square loop of side <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> and resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>7</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> is placed vertically in the east-west plane. A uniform magnetic field of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math> is set up across the plane in the north-east direction. The magnetic field is decreased to zero in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> at a steady rate. Then, the magnitude of induced emf is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msqrt><mi>x</mi></msqrt><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is _______.
solution:
The area vector for the square loop is given by

A→=(0.1)2 j^ m2

According to the above diagram, the magnetic field is given by

B→=0.22i^+0.22j^

Hence, the magnitude of induced emf can be calculated as follows

e=ΔϕΔt=B→·A→-01=0.22i^+0.22j^·(0.1)2 j^ -01 V=2×10-3 V

Hence, x=2.
